pages.raw_text:
The final curtain fell in the
great seven years struggle. be-
tween the Mine Operators associa-
ton and the Western Federation of
Miners, when the Colorado legis-
lature passed the appropriation bill
few days ago, when contained
provisions to partially reimburse
the miners who suffered damages
during the Peabody war" With
out an exception this contest was
the most bitter and uncompromis-
ing that ever has been waged be-
tween labor and capital on this
continent. It's cost mounted high
into the millions of dollars.


On the one side stood the Mine
Operators association, assisted by
Standard OILS untold wealth, the
smelter combine, railways, etc. /;
the governor, the high courts, the
militia, several detective and
strike-breaking agencies, the Cit.
liens alliance, with a few notable
exceptions the daily newspapers
of the country, and even President
Roosevelt and other Washington
officials threw their immense pow.
er and influence to the support OF
the employers.


On the other side stood the
Western Federation of Miners
with its back to the wall, grim
and determined, resolved to fight
to the last ditch to preserve its
entity and safeguard the rights of
its membership. Financially, the
miners were forced to depend
upon the small contributions of
their fellow workingmen and sym.
pathizers, and the traed union
press.


From the day that the men
struck in Colorado City, in 1902,
to compel the enforcement of the
eight-hour law in mines and
shelters, as voted by the people of
Colorado by an overwhelming ma
jority In referendum, down to
the elimination of the celebrated
trials In Boise, not single
charge laid at the doors of the
miners by the operators and their
press, politicians and Pinkertons
has been substantiated In court.
